title: Simple Small Trestle Desk
summary: A compact trestle desk from one 2x4 and two 2x6s — splayed 10° legs, a plank top, and a low shelf brace that doubles as a footrest. Pocket holes throughout.
description:
Trestle tables usually mean big joinery — through-tenons, wedges, a beam you could dock a boat to. This one keeps the trestle silhouette and none of the difficulty: two splayed leg assemblies (each pair of 2x4 legs cut at 10°, ends parallel, joined by a 2x6 apron), a top of four 2x6 planks, and an angled 2x4 shelf brace low between the trestles that stiffens the whole desk and catches your feet.

The 10° cuts do all the visual work. Long point to short point with ends PARALLEL means each leg leans outward while sitting flat on the floor and flat under the apron — the splay is what makes it read 'trestle' instead of 'table with legs.' Cut all four legs in one saw session so the angle is identical across them.

At roughly 38" wide, this is a genuinely small desk — a laptop-and-notebook station for a bedroom corner or rental apartment, not a command center. The source offers an honest upgrade path for the writing surface: construction-lumber tops have seams and knots, so a piece of acrylic or a desk mat over the planks gives a smooth writing surface without hiding the wood. Or substitute a premade panel top on the same base.

Sand the 2x6 top seams flush — pocket-screwed edge joints want a card scraper or a hard sanding block to disappear — and break the front edge generously where forearms rest.

tools.note: The 10° leg cuts — batch all four at one setting.
The top seams. Budget real time here — it's the surface you'll touch daily.

materials.name: 2x4, 10 ft
materials.note: All four legs and the shelf braces. A 9-ft wall stud also works.

materials.name: 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Aprons and top planks. Pick the two straightest, driest in the rack.

materials.name: Stain or finish
materials.note: A desk mat or acrylic sheet over the planks upgrades the writing surface — the source's suggestion.

cutList.note: Both ends 10° off square, long point to short point, ends PARALLEL — the splay.
Long point to long point, both ends 10° — they meet the splayed legs flat.

steps.title: Build the two trestles
steps.body:
Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the tops of all four legs and attach a pair to each 2x6 apron with 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue — legs splaying OUTWARD, both the same amount.

Cut all four legs at one saw setting and the trestles come out as twins. Stand both on a flat surface side by side: same footprint, same lean, no rock. Fix any disagreement now.

steps.title: Join the top and land it
steps.body:
Edge-join the four 2x6 planks with 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" screws, clamping each seam flat as you drive — a stepped seam on a writing desk gets noticed by every pen stroke.

Flip the top face-down, position the trestles, and screw up through the apron pocket holes into the top. Prefer a smooth surface? A premade panel, acrylic sheet, or desk mat over the planks all work — the base doesn't care.

steps.title: Add the shelf brace
steps.body:
Fit the two 10°-cut 2x4 braces low between the trestles with pocket holes — the angled ends land flat against the splayed legs. This low rail is the anti-rack structure for the whole desk (and where your feet will live).

Sand everything, flatten the top seams, round the front edge where wrists rest, and finish. Small desk, honest joinery, done by dinner.

images.alt: Small wooden trestle desk with splayed legs and plank top
